title: Pricing + usage review with Acme AI
preview: Acme AI flagged a discrepancy between Feb invoice preview and their internal counts/usage exports (invoice billed tokens higher). Redwood explained the current Hosted API metering semantics (billed_tokens derived from prompt+completion tokens, model tokenizer-specific; streaming bills for tokens actually generated; retries/fallback should not double-bill but there is a known edge case under certain server retry paths). Nadia confirmed they are rolling out improved dedupe/idempotency and cache_hit attribution fields, plus a "shadow billing compare" to validate changes before they affect invoices. The group agreed on a standard daily export schema (UTC) including request_id, idempotency_key, model, region, raw vs billed tokens, cache_hit fields, retry/fallback indicators, and status so Acme can reconcile per-request. Redwood to run a targeted reconciliation for 2026-02-01 through 2026-02-05 and follow up with findings and any credit recommendation if warranted.

title: Acme AI x Redwood — Post-Incident Review (Dedicated) — Jan 10 overload/5xx
preview:
Meeting: Acme AI x Redwood — Post-Incident Review (Dedicated) — Jan 10 overload/5xx
Recorded by Fireflies
Date: 2026-01-14
Time: 17:00 UTC
Duration: 52 minutes

Attendees
Redwood Inference: Rachel Kim, Rafael Mendes, Lauren Bishop, Dev Patel
Acme AI: Jordan Lee, Priya Nair, Miguel Santos, Hannah Wu

Summary (auto-generated)
- Reviewed the Jan 10 Dedicated incident impacting Acme AI (elevated 5xx, latency, and intermittent 429s during a burst window).
- Redwood walked through timeline, mitigations (admission control changes, autoscale ceiling increase, capacity rebalance), and likely root cause (insufficient headroom + routing behavior under saturation).
- Customer feedback focused on earlier warning, clearer status updates, and what remediation SLAs mean for Dedicated.
- Agreed on follow-ups: share PIR, confirm classification, align on comms templates and headroom planning.

Topics (auto-generated)
- Incident timeline and impact
- Dedicated capacity saturation signals
- Router fallback / spillover behavior
- Client retry/backoff and streaming behavior
- Remediation SLAs and customer communications
- Preventative actions and monitoring

Questions (auto-generated)
- When does the mitigation clock start for Dedicated, and what counts as “mitigated”?
- Will Redwood page Acme on-call automatically for future Sev1/Sev0 incidents?
- Can we have a consistent comms template that doesn’t over-promise root cause?

Transcript

[00:00] Rachel Kim: Alright, cool. Thanks everyone for joining. This is the post-incident review for the January 10th event on your Dedicated cluster. Goal is to walk the timeline, what we did to mitigate, what we think the root cause was, and then spend time on your concerns around comms and SLAs. And we’ll leave with concrete next steps.

[00:18] Jordan Lee: Sounds good.

[00:19] Priya Nair: Yep.

[00:21] Rachel Kim: Quick intros for the recording—Rachel, I run enterprise customer comms on our side. Rafael is our SRE program lead, Lauren is product for Dedicated SLAs, and Dev is support escalation mapping.

[00:33] Miguel Santos: Miguel, ML infra.

[00:35] Hannah Wu: Hannah, I’m PM on our side.

[00:37] Priya Nair: Priya, SRE.

[00:39] Jordan Lee: Jordan, platform.

[00:41] Rachel Kim: Great. Rafael, do you want to do the timeline and symptoms?

[00:46] Rafael Mendes: Yep. So, incident start as we saw it: Jan 10, 18:07 UTC, alarms fired for elevated request error rate on your Dedicated endpoint—mostly 5xx at the gateway, plus a jump in p95 and p99 latency. The first strong indicator was GPU saturation in the us-east-1 pool allocated to your cluster and queue depth building at the scheduler.

[01:12] Priya Nair: Just to anchor it, for us it was around 10:10 Pacific. We saw timeouts and then some 429s.

[01:19] Rafael Mendes: Exactly. We saw the 429s shortly after. For the record, the 429s were admission control—so not like account quota—more like the system protecting itself. But the experience is the same.

[01:32] Jordan Lee: Right, it was mixed. Some calls were failing with 5xx and some were 429. And streaming sessions were dropping too.

[01:39] Rafael Mendes: We saw streaming disconnects, yes. Not the primary symptom, but correlated when the system got overloaded.

[01:46] Rachel Kim: And we’ll come back to streaming because I know it affects your UX.

[01:49] Rafael Mendes: Timeline: at 18:10 UTC on-call declared a Sev1. At 18:13 we started mitigation—first step was raising the autoscaling ceiling for your pool and shifting spare capacity from a lower-priority internal batch workload.

[02:08] Miguel Santos: Was that manual? Like you had to go in and bump it?

[02:11] Rafael Mendes: It was manual. Today Dedicated autoscale has guardrails; it won’t exceed a cap without approval, mostly to avoid surprise cost and also because it can starve neighboring pools. We bumped the cap and requested additional nodes.

[02:27] Priya Nair: And provisioning time was…?

[02:29] Rafael Mendes: That’s part of what hurt. New GPUs weren’t instant. We had ~11 to 14 minutes before we saw meaningful relief from new capacity. During that window, the router was trying to load-balance across replicas that were all near saturation.

[02:47] Jordan Lee: That was the rough part. We had a scheduled product push and the burst was planned.

[02:52] Rachel Kim: Yep, understood.

[02:54] Rafael Mendes: At 18:18 we also changed admission control behavior—moved it from “fail late” to “fail early” basically. That reduced tail latency, but it increased 429 rate for a bit. In effect it traded long timeouts for quicker rejects.

[03:12] Priya Nair: That actually helped us. Our retry logic handles 429 better than hanging sockets.

[03:18] Miguel Santos: For streaming though, quick reject is okay but mid-stream drop is worse.

[03:23] Rafael Mendes: Agree. We think the mid-stream drops were due to the gateway timing out upstream or the runtime worker being killed under memory pressure—there was some OOM risk. I’ll be careful: we don’t have 100% proof on the OOM path; the logs were noisy. But we saw “out of memory” in a couple of workers.

[03:45] Jordan Lee: Is that like KV cache pressure? We’ve seen that before.

[03:49] Rafael Mendes: Yes—KV cache pressure, sequence length skew. (Transcription note: he said “K V”, not “key cash”, but yeah.) The burst had longer prompts than your baseline.

[04:03] Priya Nair: That’s true, we had a new feature that adds retrieval context.

[04:07] Rafael Mendes: Right. So, at 18:24 UTC we got additional capacity online, errors started dropping. By 18:31 UTC we were back within SLO for error rate, and by 18:36 tail latency was back to normal-ish. We kept monitoring until 19:05 and closed the incident at 19:12.

[04:28] Rachel Kim: So total customer-visible impact window—about 29 minutes for elevated errors/latency, with the sharpest part in the first ~15.

[04:37] Hannah Wu: That matches our notes.

[04:39] Rachel Kim: Can we talk classification? This is something we’re standardizing. Rafael, what category are we putting this under?

[04:46] Rafael Mendes: For our incident taxonomy v1, the primary incident type is “Capacity / Fleet shortage” and the subtype would be “Dedicated pool saturation / provisioning lag.” We also had contributing factors from routing—specifically “Routing/fallback behavior under overload” but we would not make routing the primary owner here.

[05:07] Jordan Lee: That’s the thing. From our perspective it felt like routing made it worse. Like it thrashed.

[05:14] Rafael Mendes: Totally fair. Let me describe what we saw. The router used health and latency to pick replicas. Under saturation, latency got weird, so it oscillated more than we’d like. But the initiating condition was simply not enough headroom for the prompt distribution in that burst.

[05:33] Priya Nair: So primary owner is your fleet team?

[05:36] Rafael Mendes: Primary owner would be Infra/Fleet for the capacity piece. Routing team is consulted on the oscillation issue.

[05:44] Rachel Kim: And for you, it changes who gets paged first and who writes the main corrective actions.

[05:49] Jordan Lee: Okay.

[05:51] Lauren Bishop: If I can jump in, from a Dedicated commitments standpoint, classification matters because we report incident volume and remediation SLA compliance by type and owner. But for you, what matters is: how quickly we mitigate, how we communicate, and what we do to prevent repeats.

[06:06] Miguel Santos: Right. Can you define “mitigate”? Because you said 18:31 we were back within SLO, but we still saw some elevated latency after.

[06:15] Lauren Bishop: Great question. The draft policy we’re rolling out—still in final approval—defines time-to-mitigate as “customer impact materially reduced and stable,” typically meaning error rate and p95 are within agreed bounds for a sustained period, not just a single datapoint. For Dedicated, we’re being explicit: mitigated is not “we found the issue,” it’s “your endpoint is serving within the operational envelope.”

[06:42] Priya Nair: And time-to-fix is like root cause fix shipped?

[06:45] Lauren Bishop: Exactly. Fix is the durable change—config, code, capacity planning, alerts—whatever prevents recurrence. That’s usually days, not minutes.

[06:56] Jordan Lee: So on Jan 10, would you call mitigation at 18:31?

[07:01] Rafael Mendes: We would tentatively call it mitigated at ~18:36 when latency stabilized. Errors were down earlier but tail was still bouncing.

[07:10] Rachel Kim: And we’ll put that explicitly in the write-up so it’s not ambiguous.

[07:14] Miguel Santos: Okay.

[07:16] Rachel Kim: Customer concerns—Jordan, you had a few bullets in email. Want to walk through them?

[07:22] Jordan Lee: Yeah. One is proactive notice. We only got a heads up after we’d already paged internally. Two is clarity: we got “elevated errors, investigating,” which is fine, but then later we got a message that implied it was “region instability” which spooked our leadership. Three is: do we get credits, and how do the SLAs apply to Dedicated.

[07:48] Rachel Kim: Yep, all fair.

[07:50] Dev Patel: On comms, we’re trying to standardize templates by incident type so we avoid over-specific claims mid-incident. Like “capacity saturation in your Dedicated pool, mitigations in progress, next update in X minutes.” Instead of guessing root cause.

[08:07] Priya Nair: Please do. The “region instability” thing made it sound like AWS was on fire.

[08:12] Rafael Mendes: That was on us. Internally we were looking at regional capacity constraints and someone used that phrasing. It wasn’t a full region outage.

[08:22] Rachel Kim: For proactive notice: today, our incident-bot posts to your shared Slack channel once we declare, but we didn’t declare until we got enough signal. That’s too slow for Dedicated customers with tight windows. A thing we can do is add a “pre-incident advisory” when we see sustained saturation risk.

[08:42] Jordan Lee: Like, “we see you ramping, we’re watching.”

[08:45] Rachel Kim: Exactly.

[08:46] Miguel Santos: Could you page us directly? We have PagerDuty.

[08:49] Rachel Kim: We can talk about options. Some customers prefer Slack + email. Direct PD paging is possible but we need to coordinate severity thresholds so we don’t page you for every blip.

[09:03] Hannah Wu: We’d want it only for Sev1 and Sev0, I think.

[09:06] Lauren Bishop: That aligns with our thinking.

[09:09] Priya Nair: Another question: were we spilling over to Hosted? Because we saw some response differences. Slight quality drift.

[09:18] Rafael Mendes: Important. For your Dedicated, your policy is “pinned model version” and “no spillover to hosted” by default. During the incident we did NOT enable spillover to hosted. What may have happened is that the router selected a different replica group running the same model but with a slightly different quantization profile. That’s on us; it should be consistent.

[09:41] Miguel Santos: That’s a big deal for us. We A/B on our side and any drift causes false positives.

[09:47] Rafael Mendes: Understood. We have an internal control plane setting to prevent profile variance across the pool; we’re going to enforce that for your cluster.

[09:57] Jordan Lee: So we can say there was no model change, but there may have been a different “profile”?

[10:02] Rafael Mendes: Correct. Same model version. Different execution profile. We’ll write it that way.

[10:10] Rachel Kim: And in customer comms we’ll avoid saying “fallback model” unless it truly was.

[10:15] Priya Nair: Good.

[10:16] Rachel Kim: Lauren, can you cover credits / SLA?

[10:20] Lauren Bishop: Yep. For Dedicated, your contract has an uptime commitment and a support response commitment. The remediation SLAs we’re rolling out are operational targets—time to mitigate/fix—used to drive internal behavior and reporting. They’re not automatically the same as contractual credits, but they should map sensibly.

[10:41] Jordan Lee: So are we eligible for credits from this event?

[10:44] Lauren Bishop: We’ll evaluate it against the SLA definition in your order form: downtime minutes and measurement method. This incident looks like partial unavailability rather than full outage; we need to quantify. Rachel and Dev will follow up with the calculation approach we use.

[11:00] Dev Patel: And we can do a post-incident letter summarizing impact and mitigations. Some customers use that for internal reporting even if credits aren’t triggered.

[11:09] Hannah Wu: That would help.

[11:11] Miguel Santos: Technical question: why didn’t autoscale kick in earlier? We had a predictable ramp.

[11:17] Rafael Mendes: Two reasons. One: the autoscaler keys off a few signals—GPU utilization, queue depth, and request rate. The request rate ramped, but the queue depth didn’t cross threshold until prompts got longer. Two: we cap autoscale for Dedicated to avoid runaway. Your cap was set based on your previous peak. You exceeded it.

[11:40] Priya Nair: So we should rebaseline the cap.

[11:43] Rafael Mendes: Yes. And we can set a “burst window” schedule that temporarily increases headroom during your known peak. That’s something Fleet can do.

[11:53] Jordan Lee: That’d be ideal. Our peak is 9 to 11 Pacific weekdays.

[11:57] Rafael Mendes: Noted.

[12:00] Miguel Santos: Also, you mentioned OOM. Does that mean we need a max tokens guardrail?

[12:05] Rafael Mendes: Potentially. You’re already at max_new_tokens=512, but total context length increased. We can add a safeguard at the gateway to reject extreme prompt sizes with a clear error before it harms the cluster.

[12:20] Priya Nair: We’d prefer a 4xx with “prompt too large” rather than random 5xx.

[12:24] Rafael Mendes: Exactly.

[12:26] Rachel Kim: On the comms side, that also gives us cleaner language: “requests exceeded configured limits.”

[12:33] Jordan Lee: Another comms point: update cadence. We got updates at like minute 0 and then minute 25.

[12:41] Rachel Kim: Yeah, that’s not acceptable for Sev1. Our draft template is every 10–15 minutes during active mitigation, even if the update is “still mitigating, no ETA.” We’ll standardize that.

[12:54] Dev Patel: And we’ll keep a “what we know / what we don’t know” section so we don’t speculate.

[13:01] Priya Nair: +1.

[13:03] Miguel Santos: Can you share the metrics you used? We only see the client side.

[13:07] Rafael Mendes: We can share a redacted snapshot: saturation, queue depth, error codes breakdown. For Dedicated, we can also expose some of this in Console, but it’s ops-only right now.

[13:21] Rachel Kim: We’re working on what’s safe to expose to CS and to customers; right now we’ll do a manual share for this incident.

[13:29] Jordan Lee: Okay.

[13:31] Rafael Mendes: I also want to address the routing thrash comment. We have a workstream to instrument “fallback reasons” and routing decisions better, so we can distinguish capacity-driven routing from policy misconfig.

[13:45] Priya Nair: Yeah, because from the outside it’s just errors.

[13:48] Rafael Mendes: Exactly. Today it’s too ambiguous.

[13:51] Lauren Bishop: This ties to remediation SLAs too. If it’s a pure fleet shortage, fix path is headroom + provisioning. If it’s routing misbehavior, fix path is code/config. Different owners.

[14:04] Jordan Lee: Makes sense.

[14:06] Rachel Kim: Let’s talk preventative actions. Rafael, what are you committing to on Redwood side?

[14:11] Rafael Mendes: Four items. One: adjust your Dedicated autoscale cap and add scheduled burst headroom. Two: enforce consistent execution profile across your pool—no silent drift. Three: implement earlier warning alerts—when we detect approaching saturation for Dedicated, we proactively notify you in Slack. Four: add a runbook + internal checklist so the on-call knows the tradeoff between failing fast (429) vs tail latency.

[14:40] Priya Nair: And streaming disconnects? Any specific fix?

[14:44] Rafael Mendes: We suspect some disconnects were gateway timeouts. We can tune the upstream idle timeout for streaming in your Dedicated gateway and ensure we’re not killing worker processes under memory pressure. I don’t want to promise full elimination without validating. But we’ll investigate.

[15:05] Miguel Santos: Our SDK upgrade around that time—could that have contributed?

[15:10] Priya Nair: We did roll a new version, yes.

[15:13] Rafael Mendes: Possibly, but the correlated saturation signals make me think server-side overload was primary. Still, if the new SDK changed retry behavior, it could amplify load. If you can share retry/backoff settings, we’ll sanity check.

[15:31] Jordan Lee: We can send that.

[15:33] Rachel Kim: Great.

[15:35] Hannah Wu: On the SLA / remediation language—can we get something we can copy/paste internally? Like definition of Sev1, mitigation, etc.

[15:44] Lauren Bishop: Yes. I’ll send the one-pager. It basically says: Severity is based on customer impact; mitigation target depends on tier; Dedicated has tighter comms expectations; exceptions include cloud provider outages, but we try to mitigate regardless.

[16:03] Jordan Lee: We’d like to know what counts as “cloud provider outage.” Because that can feel like a loophole.

[16:10] Lauren Bishop: Totally. It’s not meant as a loophole. It’s mainly for credit calculations, not for our operational urgency. Operationally, we still mitigate. The carve-out is for when the underlying cloud region is broadly impaired and we can’t provision/route around it.

[16:27] Priya Nair: In this case, it wasn’t that.

[16:29] Rafael Mendes: Correct.

[16:31] Rachel Kim: Can we talk about escalation path? During the incident, you posted in shared Slack. Did you know who to ping?

[16:38] Priya Nair: We pinged our TAM alias and then ended up in the incident channel. It worked, but it was a little chaotic.

[16:45] Dev Patel: We’re standardizing: for Dedicated Sev1/Sev0, you’ll get a single comms thread in shared Slack with an owner, and a backup email summary. And the incident commander role on our side is clear.

[17:00] Jordan Lee: That helps.

[17:02] Miguel Santos: Also, if the mitigation is “more 429s,” we want to know that explicitly so we can adjust client behavior.

[17:09] Rafael Mendes: Great call. We’ll include “expected client behavior” in comms: whether to retry, backoff, or reduce concurrency.

[17:18] Rachel Kim: That’s part of the templates.

[17:21] Priya Nair: One more technical: did you do any region failover?

[17:25] Rafael Mendes: No region failover. For your Dedicated, you’re single-region today with warm spares, but not active-active. We did not move to another region.

[17:36] Jordan Lee: Is active-active possible?

[17:38] Lauren Bishop: It’s possible as an add-on architecture, but it’s more cost. Let’s not derail today, but we can do a separate design session.

[17:48] Jordan Lee: Okay.

[17:50] Rachel Kim: I’m looking at time—we have ~10 minutes. I want to make sure we captured your top asks: earlier warning, consistent comms cadence with safer language, clarity on mitigation vs fix, and confidence there was no model fallback. Anything else?

[18:06] Priya Nair: That’s it for me.

[18:07] Miguel Santos: Just make sure the “profile drift” thing gets fixed. That’s important.

[18:12] Jordan Lee: And headroom planning.

[18:14] Rachel Kim: Great.

[18:16] Rafael Mendes: For the record, our internal follow-up will include adding better labels for incident type so we don’t end up with generic “overload” every time. This one should be “Dedicated capacity saturation.”

[18:29] Priya Nair: Yep.

[18:31] Rachel Kim: Cool. Next steps recap: Rafael will send the PIR write-up with timeline/metrics and final classification by Friday. Lauren sends the Dedicated remediation SLA one-pager and how it ties to credits evaluation. Dev sends comms template snippet and cadence expectations. And Acme will send workload forecast and retry/backoff info.

[18:55] Hannah Wu: And a follow-up meeting for the burst window config?

[18:58] Rachel Kim: Yes, we’ll schedule a 30-minute follow-up in two weeks. We’ll include Fleet as needed.

[19:05] Jordan Lee: Works.

[19:06] Priya Nair: Thanks.

[19:07] Miguel Santos: Thank you.

[19:09] Rachel Kim: Thanks everyone. We’ll get those notes out.

(Transcript ends — remainder of recording contained small talk / scheduling that was not fully transcribed.)

The draft policy we’re rolling out—still in final approval—defines time-to-mitigate as “customer impact materially reduced and stable,” typically meaning error rate and p95 are within agreed bounds for a sustained period, not just a single datapoint. For Dedicated, we’re being explicit: mitigated is not “we found the issue,” it’s “your endpoint is serving within the operational envelope.”

[06:42] Priya Nair: And time-to-fix is like root cause fix shipped?

[06:45] Lauren Bishop: Exactly. Fix is the durable change—config, code, capacity planning, alerts—whatever prevents recurrence. That’s usually days, not minutes.

[06:56] Jordan Lee: So on Jan 10, would you call mitigation at 18:31?

[07:01] Rafael Mendes: We would tentatively call it mitigated at ~18:36 when latency stabilized. Errors were down earlier but tail was still bouncing.

[07:10] Rachel Kim: And we’ll put that explicitly in the write-up so it’s not ambiguous.

[07:14] Miguel Santos: Okay.

[07:16] Rachel Kim: Customer concerns—Jordan, you had a few bullets in email. Want to walk through them?

[07:22] Jordan Lee: Yeah. One is proactive notice. We only got a heads up after we’d already paged internally. Two is clarity: we got “elevated errors, investigating,” which is fine, but then later we got a message that implied it was “region instability” which spooked our leadership. Three is: do we get credits, and how do the SLAs apply to Dedicated.

[07:48] Rachel Kim: Yep, all fair.

[07:50] Dev Patel: On comms, we’re trying to standardize templates by incident type so we avoid over-specific claims mid-incident. Like “capacity saturation in your Dedicated pool, mitigations in progress, next update in X minutes.” Instead of guessing root cause.

[08:07] Priya Nair: Please do. The “region instability” thing made it sound like AWS was on fire.

[08:12] Rafael Mendes: That was on us. Internally we were looking at regional capacity constraints and someone used that phrasing. It wasn’t a full region outage.

[08:22] Rachel Kim: For proactive notice: today, our incident-bot posts to your shared Slack channel once we declare, but we didn’t declare until we got enough signal. That’s too slow for Dedicated customers with tight windows. A thing we can do is add a “pre-incident advisory” when we see sustained saturation risk.

[08:42] Jordan Lee: Like, “we see you ramping, we’re watching.”

[08:45] Rachel Kim: Exactly.

[08:46] Miguel Santos: Could you page us directly? We have PagerDuty.

[08:49] Rachel Kim: We can talk about options. Some customers prefer Slack + email. Direct PD paging is possible but we need to coordinate severity thresholds so we don’t page you for every blip.

[09:03] Hannah Wu: We’d want it only for Sev1 and Sev0, I think.

[09:06] Lauren Bishop: That aligns with our thinking.

[09:09] Priya Nair: Another question: were we spilling over to Hosted? Because we saw some response differences. Slight quality drift.

[09:18] Rafael Mendes: Important. For your Dedicated, your policy is “pinned model version” and “no spillover to hosted” by default. During the incident we did NOT enable spillover to hosted. What may have happened is that the router selected a different replica group running the same model but with a slightly different quantization profile. That’s on us; it should be consistent.

[09:41] Miguel Santos: That’s a big deal for us. We A/B on our side and any drift causes false positives.

[09:47] Rafael Mendes: Understood. We have an internal control plane setting to prevent profile variance across the pool; we’re going to enforce that for your cluster.

[09:57] Jordan Lee: So we can say there was no model change, but there may have been a different “profile”?

[10:02] Rafael Mendes: Correct. Same model version. Different execution profile. We’ll write it that way.

[10:10] Rachel Kim: And in customer comms we’ll avoid saying “fallback model” unless it truly was.

[10:15] Priya Nair: Good.

[10:16] Rachel Kim: Lauren, can you cover credits / SLA?

[10:20] Lauren Bishop: Yep. For Dedicated, your contract has an uptime commitment and a support response commitment. The remediation SLAs we’re rolling out are operational targets—time to mitigate/fix—used to drive internal behavior and reporting. They’re not automatically the same as contractual credits, but they should map sensibly.

[10:41] Jordan Lee: So are we eligible for credits from this event?

[10:44] Lauren Bishop: We’ll evaluate it against the SLA definition in your order form: downtime minutes and measurement method. This incident looks like partial unavailability rather than full outage; we need to quantify. Rachel and Dev will follow up with the calculation approach we use.

[11:00] Dev Patel: And we can do a post-incident letter summarizing impact and mitigations. Some customers use that for internal reporting even if credits aren’t triggered.

[11:09] Hannah Wu: That would help.

[11:11] Miguel Santos: Technical question: why didn’t autoscale kick in earlier? We had a predictable ramp.

[11:17] Rafael Mendes: Two reasons. One: the autoscaler keys off a few signals—GPU utilization, queue depth, and request rate. The request rate ramped, but the queue depth didn’t cross threshold until prompts got longer. Two: we cap autoscale for Dedicated to avoid runaway. Your cap was set based on your previous peak. You exceeded it.

[11:40] Priya Nair: So we should rebaseline the cap.

[11:43] Rafael Mendes: Yes. And we can set a “burst window” schedule that temporarily increases headroom during your known peak. That’s something Fleet can do.

[11:53] Jordan Lee: That’d be ideal. Our peak is 9 to 11 Pacific weekdays.

[11:57] Rafael Mendes: Noted.

[12:00] Miguel Santos: Also, you mentioned OOM. Does that mean we need a max tokens guardrail?

[12:05] Rafael Mendes: Potentially. You’re already at max_new_tokens=512, but total context length increased. We can add a safeguard at the gateway to reject extreme prompt sizes with a clear error before it harms the cluster.

[12:20] Priya Nair: We’d prefer a 4xx with “prompt too large” rather than random 5xx.

[12:24] Rafael Mendes: Exactly.

[12:26] Rachel Kim: On the comms side, that also gives us cleaner language: “requests exceeded configured limits.”

[12:33] Jordan Lee: Another comms point: update cadence. We got updates at like minute 0 and then minute 25.

[12:41] Rachel Kim: Yeah, that’s not acceptable for Sev1. Our draft template is every 10–15 minutes during active mitigation, even if the update is “still mitigating, no ETA.” We’ll standardize that.

[12:54] Dev Patel: And we’ll keep a “what we know / what we don’t know” section so we don’t speculate.

[13:01] Priya Nair: +1.

[13:03] Miguel Santos: Can you share the metrics you used? We only see the client side.

[13:07] Rafael Mendes: We can share a redacted snapshot: saturation, queue depth, error codes breakdown. For Dedicated, we can also expose some of this in Console, but it’s ops-only right now.

[13:21] Rachel Kim: We’re working on what’s safe to expose to CS and to customers; right now we’ll do a manual share for this incident.

[13:29] Jordan Lee: Okay.

[13:31] Rafael Mendes: I also want to address the routing thrash comment. We have a workstream to instrument “fallback reasons” and routing decisions better, so we can distinguish capacity-driven routing from policy misconfig.

[13:45] Priya Nair: Yeah, because from the outside it’s just errors.

[13:48] Rafael Mendes: Exactly. Today it’s too ambiguous.

[13:51] Lauren Bishop: This ties to remediation SLAs too. If it’s a pure fleet shortage, fix path is headroom + provisioning. If it’s routing misbehavior, fix path is code/config. Different owners.

[14:04] Jordan Lee: Makes sense.

[14:06] Rachel Kim: Let’s talk preventative actions. Rafael, what are you committing to on Redwood side?

[14:11] Rafael Mendes: Four items. One: adjust your Dedicated autoscale cap and add scheduled burst headroom. Two: enforce consistent execution profile across your pool—no silent drift. Three: implement earlier warning alerts—when we detect approaching saturation for Dedicated, we proactively notify you in Slack. Four: add a runbook + internal checklist so the on-call knows the tradeoff between failing fast (429) vs tail latency.

[14:40] Priya Nair: And streaming disconnects? Any specific fix?

[14:44] Rafael Mendes: We suspect some disconnects were gateway timeouts. We can tune the upstream idle timeout for streaming in your Dedicated gateway and ensure we’re not killing worker processes under memory pressure. I don’t want to promise full elimination without validating. But we’ll investigate.

[15:05] Miguel Santos: Our SDK upgrade around that time—could that have contributed?

[15:10] Priya Nair: We did roll a new version, yes.

[15:13] Rafael Mendes: Possibly, but the correlated saturation signals make me think server-side overload was primary. Still, if the new SDK changed retry behavior, it could amplify load. If you can share retry/backoff settings, we’ll sanity check.

[15:31] Jordan Lee: We can send that.

[15:33] Rachel Kim: Great.

[15:35] Hannah Wu: On the SLA / remediation language—can we get something we can copy/paste internally? Like definition of Sev1, mitigation, etc.

[15:44] Lauren Bishop: Yes. I’ll send the one-pager. It basically says: Severity is based on customer impact; mitigation target depends on tier; Dedicated has tighter comms expectations; exceptions include cloud provider outages, but we try to mitigate regardless.

[16:03] Jordan Lee: We’d like to know what counts as “cloud provider outage.” Because that can feel like a loophole.

[16:10] Lauren Bishop: Totally. It’s not meant as a loophole. It’s mainly for credit calculations, not for our operational urgency. Operationally, we still mitigate. The carve-out is for when the underlying cloud region is broadly impaired and we can’t provision/route around it.

[16:27] Priya Nair: In this case, it wasn’t that.

[16:29] Rafael Mendes: Correct.

[16:31] Rachel Kim: Can we talk about escalation path? During the incident, you posted in shared Slack. Did you know who to ping?

[16:38] Priya Nair: We pinged our TAM alias and then ended up in the incident channel. It worked, but it was a little chaotic.

[16:45] Dev Patel: We’re standardizing: for Dedicated Sev1/Sev0, you’ll get a single comms thread in shared Slack with an owner, and a backup email summary. And the incident commander role on our side is clear.

[17:00] Jordan Lee: That helps.

[17:02] Miguel Santos: Also, if the mitigation is “more 429s,” we want to know that explicitly so we can adjust client behavior.

[17:09] Rafael Mendes: Great call. We’ll include “expected client behavior” in comms: whether to retry, backoff, or reduce concurrency.

[17:18] Rachel Kim: That’s part of the templates.

[17:21] Priya Nair: One more technical: did you do any region failover?

[17:25] Rafael Mendes: No region failover. For your Dedicated, you’re single-region today with warm spares, but not active-active. We did not move to another region.

[17:36] Jordan Lee: Is active-active possible?

[17:38] Lauren Bishop: It’s possible as an add-on architecture, but it’s more cost. Let’s not derail today, but we can do a separate design session.

[17:48] Jordan Lee: Okay.

[17:50] Rachel Kim: I’m looking at time—we have ~10 minutes. I want to make sure we captured your top asks: earlier warning, consistent comms cadence with safer language, clarity on mitigation vs fix, and confidence there was no model fallback. Anything else?

[18:06] Priya Nair: That’s it for me.

[18:07] Miguel Santos: Just make sure the “profile drift” thing gets fixed. That’s important.

[18:12] Jordan Lee: And headroom planning.

[18:14] Rachel Kim: Great.

[18:16] Rafael Mendes: For the record, our internal follow-up will include adding better labels for incident type so we don’t end up with generic “overload” every time. This one should be “Dedicated capacity saturation.”

[18:29] Priya Nair: Yep.

[18:31] Rachel Kim: Cool. Next steps recap: Rafael will send the PIR write-up with timeline/metrics and final classification by Friday. Lauren sends the Dedicated remediation SLA one-pager and how it ties to credits evaluation. Dev sends comms template snippet and cadence expectations. And Acme will send workload forecast and retry/backoff info.

[18:55] Hannah Wu: And a follow-up meeting for the burst window config?

[18:58] Rachel Kim: Yes, we’ll schedule a 30-minute follow-up in two weeks. We’ll include Fleet as needed.

[19:05] Jordan Lee: Works.

[19:06] Priya Nair: Thanks.

[19:07] Miguel Santos: Thank you.

[19:09] Rachel Kim: Thanks everyone. We’ll get those notes out.

(Transcript ends — remainder of recording contained small talk / scheduling that was not fully transcribed.)

title: Acme AI - incident review + diagnostics gaps (deploy/config/saturation)
preview: Acme AI walked Redwood through the 3/10 incident impact (p95 latency spike + intermittent 503s on a subset of routes) and the biggest diagnostic gaps: (1) no easy “what deployed recently” context tied to the incident thread, (2) config/routing changes were hard to confirm quickly and safely (feature flags, model pins, quota/rate-limit) without manually paging multiple teams, and (3) saturation signals were inconsistent (GPU util looked fine, but queue depth + KV cache pressure were elevated). Redwood shared planned Incident Bot v2 context cards (recent deploys, tenant-safe config diffs with redaction, saturation signals + deep links), plus constraints around permissioning/audit logging and query budgets. Agreed on a short beta for Acme’s on-call runbooks and which dashboards/links would be most useful.

title: POC check-in - stability + rollout plan (Acme AI)
preview:
Fireflies.ai
Meeting Transcript

Title: POC check-in - stability + rollout plan (Acme AI)
Date: 2025-02-25
Time: 16:00 UTC
Duration: 43 min

Attendees
Redwood Inference: Rachel Kim, Liam O'Rourke, Noah Patel, Rafael Mendes
Acme AI: Priya Shah, Daniel Cho, Maya Chen, Alex Nguyen

Summary (auto-generated)
- Reviewed results from last week’s mitigations (concurrency cap + batch sizing guardrails) and early canary signals for TP stability fixes.
- Discussed runtime 1.19 rollout sequencing: Dedicated canary → expanded canary → production.
- Aligned on success criteria for Acme: reduced request timeouts, no “stuck stream” behavior, and stable p99 latency under mixed prefill/decode.
- Identified remaining risk areas: client-cancel path during streaming, NCCL stall signatures, and false positive watchdog aborts.

Topics (auto-generated)
1) Current state: timeout rate and p99 since mitigations
2) Canary readout: what improved / what still looks weird
3) Rollout plan and feature flags (watchdog, scheduler guardrail)
4) Success criteria + what data Redwood needs from Acme
5) Next steps / go-no go

Questions (auto-generated)
- Will the watchdog abort cause customer-visible errors? Is there retry behavior?
- What is the rollback trigger if p99 regresses but timeouts go down?
- Can Acme run with extra NCCL debug logging safely?

Transcript

[00:00] Rachel Kim: Cool, I think we’re recording. Um thanks everyone for jumping on. This is our follow-up from last week’s deep dive on the TP hangs. Goal today is basically: (1) are the mitigations helping, (2) what did we see in the canary, and (3) agree on rollout timeline and success criteria.

[00:18] Priya Shah: Sounds good.

[00:20] Liam O'Rourke: Hey all.

[00:22] Noah Patel: Hi.

[00:23] Rafael Mendes: Hey, Rafael from SRE.

[00:26] Daniel Cho: Daniel here.

[00:27] Alex Nguyen: Alex, ML.

[00:29] Maya Chen: Maya.

[00:31] Rachel Kim: Great. Priya, do you want to start with like, what you’ve seen since you applied the concurrency cap and the smaller batch? Like qualitatively and numbers if you have them.

[00:41] Priya Shah: Yeah. So we turned on the caps Friday night, basically we put the per-replica concurrency down… I think from 24 to 14? Daniel can correct me. And we also set the max prefill tokens per batch, and that reduced the like, “everything is stuck” events.

[00:59] Daniel Cho: Yeah, it was 24 to 12 actually on the busiest deployment. We did 14 on the less busy one. And the timeouts, I pulled the graph this morning. The request timeout rate dropped from around 0.8% at peak to like 0.15-ish. It’s not zero, but it’s like 5x better.

[01:17] Rachel Kim: That’s good.

[01:18] Daniel Cho: But p99 got a little worse, which makes sense because we’re throttling. Like p99 went from ~3.8 seconds to ~4.6 seconds on the chat endpoint.

[01:28] Maya Chen: Which is okay-ish for us for now, but not like… we can’t keep it there forever.

[01:34] Rachel Kim: Yeah totally. The point of the mitigation is “stop the bleeding” not the steady state.

[01:39] Noah Patel: And the pattern you’re describing matches what we’ve seen. The hang signature is triggered under a certain mix, so reducing concurrency reduces probability, but it also increases queuing.

[01:50] Priya Shah: Right.

[01:51] Rachel Kim: So that’s the baseline. Now, on our side, we shipped a canary build to a small set of Dedicated clusters last week with the scheduling guardrail and additional NCCL stream sync. We haven’t rolled it broad on Hosted yet. For Acme, we can do a canary on one replica group, TP=4, A100 80 gig, same as production.

[02:12] Alex Nguyen: That’s the same hardware we have, yeah.

[02:15] Rachel Kim: Noah, do you want to summarize what changed in the canary, in plain English.

[02:20] Noah Patel: Sure. So, three buckets. One: scheduler changes so collectives can’t be starved by prefill-heavy batches. Before, under certain interleavings, rank 0 could get ahead on compute and then wait on a collective that other ranks never reach because they’re stuck doing other work, and it looks like a “silent stall.” Two: stricter stream/event ordering around NCCL calls; some paths relied on implicit sync, which is not always safe especially with multi-stream decode. Three: instrumentation—heartbeat per rank, in-flight collective counters, and stall reason tags. And then the optional thing, which we haven’t defaulted on, is the watchdog timeout that aborts the step and drains the replica instead of waiting forever.

[03:04] Daniel Cho: When you say drains the replica, does that mean it kills the pod?

[03:08] Rafael Mendes: Not necessarily kill, but it removes it from service and we replace it. Practically, yes, you’ll see a drain event and then a restart depending on the environment policy.

[03:17] Daniel Cho: Ok.

[03:18] Maya Chen: And does that mean end-user sees an error?

[03:21] Rachel Kim: Potentially, yes, but a fast error rather than a 60-second hang then timeout. And we can route or retry at your client layer depending how you’re set up.

[03:30] Noah Patel: The idea is “fail closed” with a structured error code. It should be actionable: “tp_stall_watchdog_abort” basically, rather than generic 504.

[03:40] Priya Shah: That’s better than the long wait. We can handle retries if it’s not too frequent.

[03:45] Rachel Kim: Exactly. So, canary results so far. Rafael, do you want to share what SRE saw on the Dedicated canary clusters?

[03:53] Rafael Mendes: Yep. On the two clusters we canaried—different customers, similar workloads—we saw timeouts drop materially. On one, request timeout rate during peak went from ~0.6% to ~0.05%. The big thing is the heartbeat gaps basically disappeared. We still saw occasional NCCL errors, but those are now surfaced and correlate with a drain event. So instead of the cluster getting into a wedged state for like 20 minutes, it recovers in, say, under 2 minutes.

[04:23] Daniel Cho: That’s good.

[04:24] Rafael Mendes: Perf impact: there was a small regression, like 1 to 2% throughput down, because of extra sync. But it’s within our error bars and, honestly, worth it for stability.

[04:36] Alex Nguyen: And did you run long context? We have like 16k prompts sometimes.

[04:41] Noah Patel: We did. The canary included long-context stress scenario. The hang signature was more likely with mixed long prefill and then streaming decode on other requests. With the scheduler guardrail, we didn’t reproduce the “decode never returns” issue.

[04:56] Priya Shah: So you’re saying it’s fixed.

[04:58] Noah Patel: I’m saying the top two signatures we’re targeting appear mitigated. I won’t say “all hangs are fixed” because TP is… you know. But the known failure modes are improved and we have detection now.

[05:10] Rachel Kim: Priya, Maya, what you care about is: will your customer-facing timeouts stop. Our confidence is higher now.

[05:18] Maya Chen: Ok. What’s the rollout timeline if we agree to canary.

[05:23] Rachel Kim: Proposal: we enable canary for you tomorrow on one replica group—like 10% of traffic or one deployment slice—run it for 24 hours including a peak. If results look good Thursday, we expand to 50% Friday, then full production early next week. That’s assuming no freeze window on your side.

[05:44] Priya Shah: We do have a release freeze starting next Thursday for our own product. So ideally we’d like it in before that or we wait.

[05:52] Rachel Kim: Got it. So we aim to be in prod by Monday/Tuesday.

[05:56] Daniel Cho: How risky is this? Like what’s the rollback plan.

[06:00] Rafael Mendes: Rollback is straightforward: we can revert the runtime version for that deployment slice and/or disable the feature flags. The biggest question is if the watchdog causes false positives and drains too aggressively.

[06:12] Daniel Cho: Right, that’s what I’m worried about. If it drains constantly, we’ll get flapping.

[06:17] Noah Patel: That’s why we don’t want it default-on at first. We can start with instrumentation + scheduler + sync, and leave watchdog off. Then if you still have a small residual hang rate, we can enable watchdog with conservative thresholds.

[06:32] Maya Chen: So without watchdog, you think timeouts go away?

[06:35] Noah Patel: For the hang signatures we saw, yes. Watchdog is more like a safety net for unknowns.

[06:41] Priya Shah: Ok. That seems reasonable.

[06:44] Rachel Kim: Daniel, when you say timeouts still exist at 0.15%… do you know if they are all the same pattern? Like do you see GPU stall, or could be client cancellations.

[06:55] Daniel Cho: That’s actually unclear. Some are client disconnect, because streaming. But some are server side 504s. We didn’t have enough instrumentation to tell if it’s NCCL or scheduler.

[07:06] Noah Patel: With the new metrics, you’ll be able to tell. Like you’ll see “stall_reason=comms” vs “stall_reason=scheduler_starvation” vs “compute_long” etc.

[07:15] Alex Nguyen: Are those in the console or do we have to scrape Prometheus.

[07:19] Rachel Kim: For Dedicated, you’ll see them in the Redwood Console dashboards we share with you, and we can also export. Rafael can confirm the exact panels.

[07:27] Rafael Mendes: Yeah, we can add you to the dashboard view. There’s a panel for “TP heartbeat gap seconds” and another for “in-flight collectives” which is basically a counter per rank. If one rank’s counter stops moving, that’s the smoking gun.

[07:42] Daniel Cho: Perfect.

[07:43] Maya Chen: Can we talk success criteria. Like what do we agree means “go.”

[07:48] Rachel Kim: Yes. I drafted something but let’s align. My suggestion: during peak hour window, request timeout rate below 0.05% on the canary slice, and no sustained heartbeat gaps over, say, 30 seconds. Also p99 not worse than today’s mitigated baseline, ideally improves when we relax concurrency caps.

[08:08] Priya Shah: That’s fine. We also care about “stuck streams,” like the client waiting and then nothing.

[08:14] Noah Patel: Those should show up as heartbeat gaps or as “decode step exceeded” if watchdog is on.

[08:20] Daniel Cho: For p99, I’d like to get back under 4 seconds. The cap pushes it above.

[08:25] Rachel Kim: Yep. And part of the plan is: after stability is improved, we can increase concurrency gradually. We don’t need to go back to 24 immediately.

[08:36] Alex Nguyen: Also we need to confirm no quality changes. This is runtime not model, but we had one case with another vendor where tokens got weird.

[08:44] Noah Patel: This shouldn’t change model outputs; it’s scheduling and synchronization. But if you see anything odd, let us know.

[08:53] Maya Chen: Ok.

[08:55] Rachel Kim: Quick question: are you still doing TP=4 for all models? Or do you have a TP=2 slice.

[09:01] Priya Shah: For production it’s TP=4, single replica group per region. We tested TP=2 but throughput wasn’t enough.

[09:10] Rachel Kim: Ok.

[09:11] Daniel Cho: Another question: you mentioned extra NCCL logging. Do we need that for the canary.

[09:18] Rafael Mendes: Not unless we see something new. We can enable it for a short window if there’s an incident, but it’s noisy and has some overhead. We’ve been trying to keep it minimal.

[09:30] Daniel Cho: Ok.

[09:31] Rachel Kim: Let’s talk about the canary setup. Liam, do you want to cover how we route traffic.

[09:36] Liam O'Rourke: Sure. We can do it a couple ways. Easiest is: new runtime version on one deployment slice, then route a fixed percentage of requests by a hash. We can also do “shadow” but that’s more complicated. For what we’re doing—stability—you want real traffic.

[09:54] Priya Shah: Real traffic, yes.

[09:56] Liam O'Rourke: So we’ll do 10% by hash to canary, and you’ll be able to compare metrics side-by-side.

[10:04] Daniel Cho: Ok.

[10:06] Rachel Kim: If you’re good with it, we can start tomorrow morning your time.

[10:10] Priya Shah: Works.

[10:11] Maya Chen: Can you also share in writing what exactly changed. Like release notes.

[10:16] Noah Patel: Yeah. It’s basically: scheduler guardrail to prevent collective starvation; NCCL stream/event sync hardening; new metrics; and improved error surfacing. Watchdog is behind a flag.

[10:30] Rachel Kim: I’ll send a recap email with those bullets and the success criteria.

[10:35] Priya Shah: Great.

[10:36] Daniel Cho: I want to ask about the client cancel thing. We had some customers cancel requests in the UI. Could that trigger the hang.

[10:45] Noah Patel: Yes, we found a cancellation path bug in TP where one rank could exit early and another rank still expects a collective. That’s one of the fixes in this line of work. I believe it’s in the 1.19 bundle.

[10:59] Daniel Cho: Ok.

[11:00] Alex Nguyen: So do we need to change our client behavior.

[11:03] Noah Patel: Ideally no. Cancellation should be safe. But if you’re aggressively cancelling every token, that’s… unusual. How often do you see cancels.

[11:12] Daniel Cho: It’s like user navigates away, or they hit stop generation.

[11:16] Noah Patel: That should be fine.

[11:19] Rachel Kim: On the timeouts: you mentioned 0.15% after caps. If we canary tomorrow and see it drop further without raising p99, that’s a win.

[11:31] Maya Chen: Do we plan to remove the caps immediately.

[11:34] Rachel Kim: Not immediately. We’ll keep them during canary for apples-to-apples. Then once canary is stable, we raise concurrency in steps. Like 12 → 14 → 16, watching the stall metrics.

[11:47] Priya Shah: That’s sensible.

[11:50] Rafael Mendes: And from on-call perspective, we’ll also have new alerts. So if there is a stall signal, we drain the replica proactively instead of waiting for customer timeouts.

[12:01] Daniel Cho: That’s the piece we wanted.

[12:04] Rachel Kim: Any concerns from your side about doing the canary tomorrow.

[12:09] Priya Shah: Only the freeze timeline. But if we’re in prod by Monday, that’s fine.

[12:13] Maya Chen: Yeah.

[12:15] Alex Nguyen: We’ll need access to the dashboard.

[12:17] Rafael Mendes: I’ll coordinate that with you after this call.

[12:20] Rachel Kim: Ok. I also want to set expectations: even with the fixes, you may see an occasional drain event. But it should be rare and not cascade.

[12:30] Daniel Cho: Define rare.

[12:32] Noah Patel: In the canary we saw like, single digits per day at worst, and those were mostly correlated with real NCCL errors rather than stalls. The stalls went down drastically.

[12:44] Priya Shah: Ok.

[12:45] Maya Chen: And if we see drain events, does that show as a brief blip.

[12:49] Liam O'Rourke: If you have enough replicas, it’s masked. If you have one replica group with limited redundancy, you might see a small latency spike. But better than a long wedge.

[13:02] Priya Shah: Understood.

[13:04] Rachel Kim: Let’s do a quick recap of what we need from Acme during the canary. Daniel, we’ll ask for: request timeout rate, p95/p99, and if possible count of client cancels.

[13:16] Daniel Cho: Yes.

[13:17] Rachel Kim: And then if any errors happen, please paste the structured error code and request id so we can correlate.

[13:24] Daniel Cho: Ok.

[13:26] Rafael Mendes: Also if you can note when your peak traffic is—like hour window—so we can eyeball the graphs at the same time.

[13:34] Daniel Cho: Peak is 9am to 11am Pacific.

[13:38] Rafael Mendes: Great.

[13:39] Rachel Kim: Cool.

[13:41] Maya Chen: One more question: do you think this solves the enterprise customer impact we had last week. Like are we still at risk of a sev1.

[13:49] Noah Patel: Risk is reduced significantly. The main issue was indefinite stalls in TP that led to timeouts and autoscaling thrash. With ordering fixes and instrumentation, we can prevent and detect it. Watchdog is the backstop.

[14:05] Priya Shah: Ok.

[14:06] Rachel Kim: There’s also the question of rolling this to Hosted eventually, but for you this is Dedicated. We’ll do Dedicated-first.

[14:13] Maya Chen: Good.

[14:15] Rachel Kim: Alright, I’m going to skip ahead a bit—Noah, anything else you want them to know about the canary behavior.

[14:21] Noah Patel: Only that if you see a small throughput regression, like 1-2%, it’s expected due to stream sync. We can try to conditionalize later, but for now stability > micro perf.

[14:33] Alex Nguyen: Ok.

[14:34] Priya Shah: That’s fine.

[14:36] Rachel Kim: Great. Let’s do next steps and then we can give you time back.

[14:41] Rachel Kim: Next steps: I send recap today with success criteria and schedule. Tomorrow: canary enabled 10% with the 1.19 build, watchdog off by default. Thursday: review results. Friday: expand if good. Monday: full rollout pending go/no-go.

[14:59] Priya Shah: Sounds right.

[15:01] Daniel Cho: Works.

[15:03] Rafael Mendes: I’ll make sure you have dashboard access and I’ll be on point during your peak window tomorrow.

[15:08] Maya Chen: Thank you.

[15:10] Liam O'Rourke: I’ll coordinate the actual traffic split change with our release folks.

[15:14] Rachel Kim: Awesome. Any last questions.

[15:17] Daniel Cho: Not from me.

[15:18] Priya Shah: All good.

[15:20] Maya Chen: Thanks.

[15:21] Rachel Kim: Great, thanks everyone.

[15:24] (Call continues with a few minutes of scheduling and side chatter; transcript ends.)

Notes
- Acme confirmed current mitigations (lower concurrency + batch guardrails) reduced timeouts ~5x but increased p99 latency.
- Redwood proposed canary tomorrow; focus on stability fixes without enabling watchdog initially.
- Agreed success criteria: timeout rate <0.05% at peak on canary slice, no sustained TP heartbeat gaps, and path to bring p99 back <4s by raising concurrency after stability is proven.
text:
Fireflies.ai
Meeting Transcript

Title: POC check-in - stability + rollout plan (Acme AI)
Date: 2025-02-25
Time: 16:00 UTC
Duration: 43 min

Attendees
Redwood Inference: Rachel Kim, Liam O'Rourke, Noah Patel, Rafael Mendes
Acme AI: Priya Shah, Daniel Cho, Maya Chen, Alex Nguyen

Summary (auto-generated)
- Reviewed results from last week’s mitigations (concurrency cap + batch sizing guardrails) and early canary signals for TP stability fixes.
- Discussed runtime 1.19 rollout sequencing: Dedicated canary → expanded canary → production.
- Aligned on success criteria for Acme: reduced request timeouts, no “stuck stream” behavior, and stable p99 latency under mixed prefill/decode.
- Identified remaining risk areas: client-cancel path during streaming, NCCL stall signatures, and false positive watchdog aborts.

Topics (auto-generated)
1) Current state: timeout rate and p99 since mitigations
2) Canary readout: what improved / what still looks weird
3) Rollout plan and feature flags (watchdog, scheduler guardrail)
4) Success criteria + what data Redwood needs from Acme
5) Next steps / go-no go

Questions (auto-generated)
- Will the watchdog abort cause customer-visible errors? Is there retry behavior?
- What is the rollback trigger if p99 regresses but timeouts go down?
- Can Acme run with extra NCCL debug logging safely?

Transcript

[00:00] Rachel Kim: Cool, I think we’re recording. Um thanks everyone for jumping on. This is our follow-up from last week’s deep dive on the TP hangs. Goal today is basically: (1) are the mitigations helping, (2) what did we see in the canary, and (3) agree on rollout timeline and success criteria.

[00:18] Priya Shah: Sounds good.

[00:20] Liam O'Rourke: Hey all.

[00:22] Noah Patel: Hi.

[00:23] Rafael Mendes: Hey, Rafael from SRE.

[00:26] Daniel Cho: Daniel here.

[00:27] Alex Nguyen: Alex, ML.

[00:29] Maya Chen: Maya.

[00:31] Rachel Kim: Great. Priya, do you want to start with like, what you’ve seen since you applied the concurrency cap and the smaller batch? Like qualitatively and numbers if you have them.

[00:41] Priya Shah: Yeah. So we turned on the caps Friday night, basically we put the per-replica concurrency down… I think from 24 to 14? Daniel can correct me. And we also set the max prefill tokens per batch, and that reduced the like, “everything is stuck” events.

[00:59] Daniel Cho: Yeah, it was 24 to 12 actually on the busiest deployment. We did 14 on the less busy one. And the timeouts, I pulled the graph this morning. The request timeout rate dropped from around 0.8% at peak to like 0.15-ish. It’s not zero, but it’s like 5x better.

[01:17] Rachel Kim: That’s good.

[01:18] Daniel Cho: But p99 got a little worse, which makes sense because we’re throttling. Like p99 went from ~3.8 seconds to ~4.6 seconds on the chat endpoint.

[01:28] Maya Chen: Which is okay-ish for us for now, but not like… we can’t keep it there forever.

[01:34] Rachel Kim: Yeah totally. The point of the mitigation is “stop the bleeding” not the steady state.

[01:39] Noah Patel: And the pattern you’re describing matches what we’ve seen. The hang signature is triggered under a certain mix, so reducing concurrency reduces probability, but it also increases queuing.

[01:50] Priya Shah: Right.

[01:51] Rachel Kim: So that’s the baseline. Now, on our side, we shipped a canary build to a small set of Dedicated clusters last week with the scheduling guardrail and additional NCCL stream sync. We haven’t rolled it broad on Hosted yet. For Acme, we can do a canary on one replica group, TP=4, A100 80 gig, same as production.

[02:12] Alex Nguyen: That’s the same hardware we have, yeah.

[02:15] Rachel Kim: Noah, do you want to summarize what changed in the canary, in plain English.

[02:20] Noah Patel: Sure. So, three buckets. One: scheduler changes so collectives can’t be starved by prefill-heavy batches. Before, under certain interleavings, rank 0 could get ahead on compute and then wait on a collective that other ranks never reach because they’re stuck doing other work, and it looks like a “silent stall.” Two: stricter stream/event ordering around NCCL calls; some paths relied on implicit sync, which is not always safe especially with multi-stream decode. Three: instrumentation—heartbeat per rank, in-flight collective counters, and stall reason tags. And then the optional thing, which we haven’t defaulted on, is the watchdog timeout that aborts the step and drains the replica instead of waiting forever.

[03:04] Daniel Cho: When you say drains the replica, does that mean it kills the pod?

[03:08] Rafael Mendes: Not necessarily kill, but it removes it from service and we replace it. Practically, yes, you’ll see a drain event and then a restart depending on the environment policy.

[03:17] Daniel Cho: Ok.

[03:18] Maya Chen: And does that mean end-user sees an error?

[03:21] Rachel Kim: Potentially, yes, but a fast error rather than a 60-second hang then timeout. And we can route or retry at your client layer depending how you’re set up.

[03:30] Noah Patel: The idea is “fail closed” with a structured error code. It should be actionable: “tp_stall_watchdog_abort” basically, rather than generic 504.

[03:40] Priya Shah: That’s better than the long wait. We can handle retries if it’s not too frequent.

[03:45] Rachel Kim: Exactly. So, canary results so far. Rafael, do you want to share what SRE saw on the Dedicated canary clusters?

[03:53] Rafael Mendes: Yep. On the two clusters we canaried—different customers, similar workloads—we saw timeouts drop materially. On one, request timeout rate during peak went from ~0.6% to ~0.05%. The big thing is the heartbeat gaps basically disappeared. We still saw occasional NCCL errors, but those are now surfaced and correlate with a drain event. So instead of the cluster getting into a wedged state for like 20 minutes, it recovers in, say, under 2 minutes.

[04:23] Daniel Cho: That’s good.

[04:24] Rafael Mendes: Perf impact: there was a small regression, like 1 to 2% throughput down, because of extra sync. But it’s within our error bars and, honestly, worth it for stability.

[04:36] Alex Nguyen: And did you run long context? We have like 16k prompts sometimes.

[04:41] Noah Patel: We did. The canary included long-context stress scenario. The hang signature was more likely with mixed long prefill and then streaming decode on other requests. With the scheduler guardrail, we didn’t reproduce the “decode never returns” issue.

[04:56] Priya Shah: So you’re saying it’s fixed.

[04:58] Noah Patel: I’m saying the top two signatures we’re targeting appear mitigated. I won’t say “all hangs are fixed” because TP is… you know. But the known failure modes are improved and we have detection now.

[05:10] Rachel Kim: Priya, Maya, what you care about is: will your customer-facing timeouts stop. Our confidence is higher now.

[05:18] Maya Chen: Ok. What’s the rollout timeline if we agree to canary.

[05:23] Rachel Kim: Proposal: we enable canary for you tomorrow on one replica group—like 10% of traffic or one deployment slice—run it for 24 hours including a peak. If results look good Thursday, we expand to 50% Friday, then full production early next week. That’s assuming no freeze window on your side.

[05:44] Priya Shah: We do have a release freeze starting next Thursday for our own product. So ideally we’d like it in before that or we wait.

[05:52] Rachel Kim: Got it. So we aim to be in prod by Monday/Tuesday.

[05:56] Daniel Cho: How risky is this? Like what’s the rollback plan.

[06:00] Rafael Mendes: Rollback is straightforward: we can revert the runtime version for that deployment slice and/or disable the feature flags. The biggest question is if the watchdog causes false positives and drains too aggressively.

[06:12] Daniel Cho: Right, that’s what I’m worried about. If it drains constantly, we’ll get flapping.

[06:17] Noah Patel: That’s why we don’t want it default-on at first. We can start with instrumentation + scheduler + sync, and leave watchdog off. Then if you still have a small residual hang rate, we can enable watchdog with conservative thresholds.

[06:32] Maya Chen: So without watchdog, you think timeouts go away?

[06:35] Noah Patel: For the hang signatures we saw, yes. Watchdog is more like a safety net for unknowns.

[06:41] Priya Shah: Ok. That seems reasonable.

[06:44] Rachel Kim: Daniel, when you say timeouts still exist at 0.15%… do you know if they are all the same pattern? Like do you see GPU stall, or could be client cancellations.

[06:55] Daniel Cho: That’s actually unclear. Some are client disconnect, because streaming. But some are server side 504s. We didn’t have enough instrumentation to tell if it’s NCCL or scheduler.

[07:06] Noah Patel: With the new metrics, you’ll be able to tell. Like you’ll see “stall_reason=comms” vs “stall_reason=scheduler_starvation” vs “compute_long” etc.

[07:15] Alex Nguyen: Are those in the console or do we have to scrape Prometheus.

[07:19] Rachel Kim: For Dedicated, you’ll see them in the Redwood Console dashboards we share with you, and we can also export. Rafael can confirm the exact panels.

[07:27] Rafael Mendes: Yeah, we can add you to the dashboard view. There’s a panel for “TP heartbeat gap seconds” and another for “in-flight collectives” which is basically a counter per rank. If one rank’s counter stops moving, that’s the smoking gun.

[07:42] Daniel Cho: Perfect.

[07:43] Maya Chen: Can we talk success criteria. Like what do we agree means “go.”

[07:48] Rachel Kim: Yes. I drafted something but let’s align. My suggestion: during peak hour window, request timeout rate below 0.05% on the canary slice, and no sustained heartbeat gaps over, say, 30 seconds. Also p99 not worse than today’s mitigated baseline, ideally improves when we relax concurrency caps.

[08:08] Priya Shah: That’s fine. We also care about “stuck streams,” like the client waiting and then nothing.

[08:14] Noah Patel: Those should show up as heartbeat gaps or as “decode step exceeded” if watchdog is on.

[08:20] Daniel Cho: For p99, I’d like to get back under 4 seconds. The cap pushes it above.

[08:25] Rachel Kim: Yep. And part of the plan is: after stability is improved, we can increase concurrency gradually. We don’t need to go back to 24 immediately.

[08:36] Alex Nguyen: Also we need to confirm no quality changes. This is runtime not model, but we had one case with another vendor where tokens got weird.

[08:44] Noah Patel: This shouldn’t change model outputs; it’s scheduling and synchronization. But if you see anything odd, let us know.

[08:53] Maya Chen: Ok.

[08:55] Rachel Kim: Quick question: are you still doing TP=4 for all models? Or do you have a TP=2 slice.

[09:01] Priya Shah: For production it’s TP=4, single replica group per region. We tested TP=2 but throughput wasn’t enough.

[09:10] Rachel Kim: Ok.

[09:11] Daniel Cho: Another question: you mentioned extra NCCL logging. Do we need that for the canary.

[09:18] Rafael Mendes: Not unless we see something new. We can enable it for a short window if there’s an incident, but it’s noisy and has some overhead. We’ve been trying to keep it minimal.

[09:30] Daniel Cho: Ok.

[09:31] Rachel Kim: Let’s talk about the canary setup. Liam, do you want to cover how we route traffic.

[09:36] Liam O'Rourke: Sure. We can do it a couple ways. Easiest is: new runtime version on one deployment slice, then route a fixed percentage of requests by a hash. We can also do “shadow” but that’s more complicated. For what we’re doing—stability—you want real traffic.

[09:54] Priya Shah: Real traffic, yes.

[09:56] Liam O'Rourke: So we’ll do 10% by hash to canary, and you’ll be able to compare metrics side-by-side.

[10:04] Daniel Cho: Ok.

[10:06] Rachel Kim: If you’re good with it, we can start tomorrow morning your time.

[10:10] Priya Shah: Works.

[10:11] Maya Chen: Can you also share in writing what exactly changed. Like release notes.

[10:16] Noah Patel: Yeah. It’s basically: scheduler guardrail to prevent collective starvation; NCCL stream/event sync hardening; new metrics; and improved error surfacing. Watchdog is behind a flag.

[10:30] Rachel Kim: I’ll send a recap email with those bullets and the success criteria.

[10:35] Priya Shah: Great.

[10:36] Daniel Cho: I want to ask about the client cancel thing. We had some customers cancel requests in the UI. Could that trigger the hang.

[10:45] Noah Patel: Yes, we found a cancellation path bug in TP where one rank could exit early and another rank still expects a collective. That’s one of the fixes in this line of work. I believe it’s in the 1.19 bundle.

[10:59] Daniel Cho: Ok.

[11:00] Alex Nguyen: So do we need to change our client behavior.

[11:03] Noah Patel: Ideally no. Cancellation should be safe. But if you’re aggressively cancelling every token, that’s… unusual. How often do you see cancels.

[11:12] Daniel Cho: It’s like user navigates away, or they hit stop generation.

[11:16] Noah Patel: That should be fine.

[11:19] Rachel Kim: On the timeouts: you mentioned 0.15% after caps. If we canary tomorrow and see it drop further without raising p99, that’s a win.

[11:31] Maya Chen: Do we plan to remove the caps immediately.

[11:34] Rachel Kim: Not immediately. We’ll keep them during canary for apples-to-apples. Then once canary is stable, we raise concurrency in steps. Like 12 → 14 → 16, watching the stall metrics.

[11:47] Priya Shah: That’s sensible.

[11:50] Rafael Mendes: And from on-call perspective, we’ll also have new alerts. So if there is a stall signal, we drain the replica proactively instead of waiting for customer timeouts.

[12:01] Daniel Cho: That’s the piece we wanted.

[12:04] Rachel Kim: Any concerns from your side about doing the canary tomorrow.

[12:09] Priya Shah: Only the freeze timeline. But if we’re in prod by Monday, that’s fine.

[12:13] Maya Chen: Yeah.

[12:15] Alex Nguyen: We’ll need access to the dashboard.

[12:17] Rafael Mendes: I’ll coordinate that with you after this call.

[12:20] Rachel Kim: Ok. I also want to set expectations: even with the fixes, you may see an occasional drain event. But it should be rare and not cascade.

[12:30] Daniel Cho: Define rare.

[12:32] Noah Patel: In the canary we saw like, single digits per day at worst, and those were mostly correlated with real NCCL errors rather than stalls. The stalls went down drastically.

[12:44] Priya Shah: Ok.

[12:45] Maya Chen: And if we see drain events, does that show as a brief blip.

[12:49] Liam O'Rourke: If you have enough replicas, it’s masked. If you have one replica group with limited redundancy, you might see a small latency spike. But better than a long wedge.

[13:02] Priya Shah: Understood.

[13:04] Rachel Kim: Let’s do a quick recap of what we need from Acme during the canary. Daniel, we’ll ask for: request timeout rate, p95/p99, and if possible count of client cancels.

[13:16] Daniel Cho: Yes.

[13:17] Rachel Kim: And then if any errors happen, please paste the structured error code and request id so we can correlate.

[13:24] Daniel Cho: Ok.

[13:26] Rafael Mendes: Also if you can note when your peak traffic is—like hour window—so we can eyeball the graphs at the same time.

[13:34] Daniel Cho: Peak is 9am to 11am Pacific.

[13:38] Rafael Mendes: Great.

[13:39] Rachel Kim: Cool.

[13:41] Maya Chen: One more question: do you think this solves the enterprise customer impact we had last week. Like are we still at risk of a sev1.

[13:49] Noah Patel: Risk is reduced significantly. The main issue was indefinite stalls in TP that led to timeouts and autoscaling thrash. With ordering fixes and instrumentation, we can prevent and detect it. Watchdog is the backstop.

[14:05] Priya Shah: Ok.

[14:06] Rachel Kim: There’s also the question of rolling this to Hosted eventually, but for you this is Dedicated. We’ll do Dedicated-first.

[14:13] Maya Chen: Good.

[14:15] Rachel Kim: Alright, I’m going to skip ahead a bit—Noah, anything else you want them to know about the canary behavior.

[14:21] Noah Patel: Only that if you see a small throughput regression, like 1-2%, it’s expected due to stream sync. We can try to conditionalize later, but for now stability > micro perf.

[14:33] Alex Nguyen: Ok.

[14:34] Priya Shah: That’s fine.

[14:36] Rachel Kim: Great. Let’s do next steps and then we can give you time back.

[14:41] Rachel Kim: Next steps: I send recap today with success criteria and schedule. Tomorrow: canary enabled 10% with the 1.19 build, watchdog off by default. Thursday: review results. Friday: expand if good. Monday: full rollout pending go/no-go.

[14:59] Priya Shah: Sounds right.

[15:01] Daniel Cho: Works.

[15:03] Rafael Mendes: I’ll make sure you have dashboard access and I’ll be on point during your peak window tomorrow.

[15:08] Maya Chen: Thank you.

[15:10] Liam O'Rourke: I’ll coordinate the actual traffic split change with our release folks.

[15:14] Rachel Kim: Awesome. Any last questions.

[15:17] Daniel Cho: Not from me.

[15:18] Priya Shah: All good.

[15:20] Maya Chen: Thanks.

[15:21] Rachel Kim: Great, thanks everyone.

[15:24] (Call continues with a few minutes of scheduling and side chatter; transcript ends.)

Notes
- Acme confirmed current mitigations (lower concurrency + batch guardrails) reduced timeouts ~5x but increased p99 latency.
- Redwood proposed canary tomorrow; focus on stability fixes without enabling watchdog initially.
- Agreed success criteria: timeout rate <0.05% at peak on canary slice, no sustained TP heartbeat gaps, and path to bring p99 back <4s by raising concurrency after stability is proven.

title: Acme AI x Redwood — Dedicated TP hang technical deep dive
preview:
Fireflies.ai Transcript

Date: 2025-02-18
Time: 18:30–19:28 UTC
Duration: 58 minutes
Meeting title: Acme AI x Redwood — Dedicated TP hang technical deep dive
Call type: technical_deep_dive

Attendees
Redwood Inference: Rachel Kim, Liam O'Rourke, Noah Patel, Hiro Tanaka, Rafael Mendes
Acme AI: Maya Chen, Ethan Brooks, Priya Nair, Jonas Keller

Summary (auto-generated)
- Acme described intermittent request hangs/timeouts on Dedicated when running tensor-parallel replicas under peak load, mostly during streaming chat workloads with mixed prompt lengths.
- Redwood walked through likely failure modes (collective ordering mismatch/starvation, stream/event sync gaps around NCCL, cancellation edge cases) and what telemetry is needed to separate compute vs comms vs scheduler stalls.
- Agreed on immediate mitigations (reduce per-replica concurrency, cap prefill, temporarily disable specific fast-paths if enabled) and a plan to capture logs in a short debug window.

Topics (auto-generated)
- Symptoms and frequency of timeouts/hangs
- Workload characteristics (prefill/decode mix, long context, bursty concurrency)
- What to capture: request IDs, replica/rank context, NCCL debug, GPU metrics
- Mitigations and canary plan for upcoming runtime fixes

Questions (auto-generated)
- Is the hang correlated with streaming client disconnects?
- Are CUDA graphs enabled and if so, is capture interacting with NCCL stream ordering?
- Does the issue reproduce on smaller TP degree or only at TP=8?

Action items (auto-generated)
- Acme to share incident windows and logs
- Redwood to share debug capture instructions + mitigation config
- Follow-up meeting after artifact review

---

Transcript

[00:00] Rachel Kim: Alright, thanks everyone. This is Rachel from Redwood CS. We pulled in Noah and Hiro from runtime and Rafael from SRE. Goal today is to understand the TP hang behavior you’re seeing on Dedicated and leave with like, a concrete list of logs and immediate mitigations.

[00:18] Maya Chen: Yep, sounds good. Thanks for jumping on. This has been pretty disruptive for us.

[00:23] Liam O'Rourke: Hey all. Liam here, I’m the technical account liaison. I’ve been tracking the support tickets. Priya, I saw your note that the timeouts line up with specific replicas.

[00:33] Priya Nair: Yeah. We can show it. Basically, some replicas just… stop responding. The service is still “up” but requests hang until client timeout.

[00:42] Noah Patel: When you say hang, is it a single request stuck, or does the whole replica stop making forward progress, like queue grows and nothing completes?

[00:50] Priya Nair: The whole replica. Queue grows. We see upstream 504s from our gateway after 120 seconds. And then we drain the replica and it recovers.

[01:01] Hiro Tanaka: Got it. Is GPU utilization flat during the hang, or pegged?

[01:05] Ethan Brooks: Flat. We see a drop. It’s not like it’s doing compute slowly. It’s like stalled.

[01:12] Noah Patel: That’s consistent with a collective wait or a stream sync deadlock. Let’s get environment specifics. What’s the hardware and TP degree?

[01:20] Ethan Brooks: A100 80GB. Eight GPUs per node. Each replica uses eight, so TP=8. We have… four replicas in the pool.

[01:32] Maya Chen: Dedicated in your us-east region. It’s isolated, no shared capacity.

[01:36] Rafael Mendes: And you’re seeing it under sustained load, or only during spikes?

[01:41] Priya Nair: Mostly spikes. Like when we get bursty traffic, it starts. But we also saw it last night under steady load with a long-context job.

[01:52] Rachel Kim: Can you share a rough frequency? Like once a day, once a week?

[01:57] Priya Nair: Last week it was twice. Since Sunday, it’s been like… daily. We had a bigger launch.

[02:05] Liam O'Rourke: And you’re mostly streaming chat, right?

[02:08] Maya Chen: Yeah. It’s chat completions, streaming. We also do some summarization calls that are non-streaming.

[02:16] Noah Patel: Do you have a sense of mix? Like prefill heavy vs decode heavy?

[02:20] Ethan Brooks: Hmm. Most of our chat prompts are medium, 1k to 4k tokens input. But we have a chunk at 12k to 16k input because we do retrieval with a lot of text.

[02:35] Maya Chen: And output is like 200 to 800 tokens, usually.

[02:40] Noah Patel: Okay. Mixed prompt lengths plus streaming decode is exactly where scheduler edge cases show up.

[02:46] Hiro Tanaka: Are you using any cancellation? Like client disconnects, you stop generation?

[02:51] Priya Nair: Yes. Users close the tab, our gateway closes the stream. We send cancel. That’s common.

[02:58] Noah Patel: Important. We’ve seen a class of bugs where cancel leaves one rank not entering the next collective, and others wait.

[03:06] Maya Chen: That sounds like what we’re seeing. Is that fixed?

[03:10] Noah Patel: We have a fix in progress, but I want to confirm with evidence. We’ll need a trace ID around a hang where there was a cancellation.

[03:18] Rafael Mendes: From an ops perspective, it’d be helpful to know: when it happens, do you see any NCCL errors? Or is it silent?

[03:25] Priya Nair: Silent. No crash. No error. That’s the frustrating part.

[03:30] Hiro Tanaka: Silent stalls are usually stream/event ordering or collective mismatch without triggering timeout.

[03:36] Jonas Keller: Quick question on logging—if you ask us to turn on debug, we need to know exactly what’s logged and retention. We can’t dump customer prompts.

[03:45] Rachel Kim: Totally. We’ll keep it to runtime-level logs, no prompt text. We can also do a short capture window and you can sanitize.

[03:54] Noah Patel: For our purposes, we need: request IDs, replica ID, and internal step markers, not content.

[04:02] Liam O'Rourke: Priya, can you describe the symptom timeline? Like do you see p99 creeping up first, then hang, or it’s sudden?

[04:10] Priya Nair: It’s pretty sudden. We get a burst, latency climbs. Then one replica goes “bad” and everything routed there times out. Our autoscaler sometimes makes it worse because it adds pressure elsewhere.

[04:23] Rafael Mendes: That matches what we’ve seen in other incidents—autoscaling thrash when one replica wedges.

[04:29] Noah Patel: What are your current per-replica concurrency settings? Like max in-flight requests.

[04:35] Ethan Brooks: I think we’re at 32 per replica. That was recommended earlier for throughput.

[04:41] Hiro Tanaka: For TP=8 and long contexts, 32 can be pretty aggressive, especially mixed prefill.

[04:47] Noah Patel: As a mitigation, we may want to drop to 16 temporarily. It’s not ideal for cost, but stability.

[04:54] Maya Chen: We can do that. But we need an estimate on impact.

[04:58] Noah Patel: Likely throughput drops, but p99 should improve and you reduce stall probability. We can also cap prefill tokens per batch.

[05:08] Ethan Brooks: Are those knobs on our side or on your side?

[05:11] Liam O'Rourke: For Dedicated, we can set them at the service config layer. We’ll send recommended values, you approve.

[05:18] Rachel Kim: We’ll frame it as an interim workaround, until runtime fixes land.

[05:24] Noah Patel: Let’s talk about model and runtime version. Which model and what runtime build are you on?

[06:00] Hiro Tanaka: Potentially. Graph capture constrains stream semantics. If something tries to do collectives outside the captured ordering, it can stall.

[06:09] Ethan Brooks: I think graphs are enabled? Not 100%.

[07:26] Priya Nair: We can grab a trace ID from our logs.

[08:02] Jonas Keller: Okay. As long as it’s time-bounded and we know where it’s stored.

If one rank ends up doing “prefill path A” while another is trying to do “decode path B” at that moment because of scheduling differences, they can disagree on the next collective and stall.

[12:14] Priya Nair: That’s preferable.

[12:17] Noah Patel: Yes, trace IDs plus timestamps are huge.

[12:21] Maya Chen: Can we talk timeline? When do we get the runtime fix?

[13:38] Noah Patel: Exactly.

[15:06] Jonas Keller: Yep.

[15:34] Priya Nair: Got it.

[16:00] Maya Chen: Thursday is fine.

[16:02] Priya Nair: Yep.

[16:04] Rachel Kim: Great. We’ll send notes and the checklist. Thanks everyone.

[15:39] Noah Patel: Concurrency reduction is the safest and quickest. It reduces the scheduler corner cases.

[15:50] Maya Chen: Okay.

[15:52] Rachel Kim: We’ll send concrete numbers. We won’t leave it vague.

[15:56] Liam O'Rourke: And we’ll schedule a follow-up. How does Thursday work?

[16:02] Priya Nair: Yep.
